saves-menu.rename-content: You are about to rename save:
saves-menu.delete-content: Are you sure, you want to delete save:

first-lesson.dial1-1: To add a library to the program we use <color=yellow>#include<library></color>
first-lesson.dial1-2: Everything inside the <color=yellow>main</color> function will be executed by the program after compilation.
first-lesson.dial1-3: <color=yellow>std::cout << "hello world"</color> command, makes the text inside double-quotes appear on the screen.
first-lesson.dial1-4: At the end of every line, with some exeptions, there needs to be a semicolon.
first-lesson.dial1-5: It's not necessary to use <color=yellow>std::</color> when you add <color=yellow>using namespace std</color> at the very top of your code
first-lesson.dial2-1: You don't have to use <color=yellow>std::</color>, if you add <color=yellow>using namespace std</color> at the top of your code.
first-lesson.dial3-1: Using the program above you can assign values to two variables and display their sum.
first-lesson.dial3-2: With the help of variables, we can store some data, which can be used later in the program
first-lesson.dial3-3: There are multiple variable types, storing diffrent types of data:
first-lesson.dial3-8: <color=yellow>bool</color> stores a logical data - true or false
first-lesson.dial4-1: <color=yellow>If</color> statement allows to execute some code under a certain condition, which we put inside the brackets
first-lesson.dial4-2: The code above shows <color=yellow>x > 0</color>, if value of variable x is greater that zero
first-lesson.dial5-1: In c++ as well as in many other programming languages, you can use mathematical operators with variables
first-lesson.dial5-2: As you can see in examples above, there are many ways to use them:
first-lesson.dial5-8: <color=yellow>F</color> - Remainder of division of x and y
first-lesson.dial6-1: When using intigers for operations such as division, the result is often a real number which needs float
first-lesson.dial6-2: Unfortunately this means that when using ints you often have to change its type to floating point number
first-lesson.dial6-3: Otherwise calculations will produce incorrect results
first-lesson.dial6-4: As you can see in example A, the result is 1 because c++ automaticaly thinks that operations with two ints should produce an int
first-lesson.dial6-5: Using method B is the best for int divisions because it forces c++ to treat these numbers as floats
first-lesson.dial6-6: But method C also works, thanks to multiplying numbers by 1.0 which is a float
first-lesson.dial6-7: As you can see in examples D and E it also works with changing floats to ints

messages.too-young: You are too young to drive a car!
messages.leave-during-lesson: You can't leave class durign lesson.
